html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: 2D Gaussian surface plot centered at origin
horizontal plane at zero derivative level in green
color gradient illustrating derivative magnitude from blue (low) to red (high)
vertical spike marker at the center peak of the x-derivative

interactions.label: Sigma
interactions.teaches: Adjusting sigma alters the scale of the Gaussian, affecting the width and sharpness of the derivative peaks.

demo_steps.title: Gaussian Surface Overview
demo_steps.narration: Here we display a 2D Gaussian surface with sigma=1. Observe the symmetry of the surface and the presence of sharp peaks in the derivative plot, which are highlighted by the peaks' color transition from blue to red.

demo_steps.title: Increasing Sigma for Broader Peaks
demo_steps.narration: As sigma is increased to 3, the Gaussian surface broadens, resulting in less steep and wider derivative peaks. This shows how a larger sigma captures broader features in data.

demo_steps.title: Highlight on Fine Details
demo_steps.narration: Reducing sigma to 0.5 sharpens the peaks, bringing fine details into focus. The derivative plot shows rapid transitions marked by closely spaced, sharp spikes.

demo_steps.title: Exploring Zero Level Plane
demo_steps.narration: The green plane represents the zero derivative level, offering a reference to gauge how the Gaussian derivative intersects this level. High peaks rise above while troughs dip below it.

plan.camera_suggestion: Overhead view centered vertically on the Gaussian peak, slightly tilted to give a clear view of the derivative spikes and zero plane.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: at most 2 compact sliders/toggles, visible near an edge with no filled panel, and must not cover important geometry
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

plan.notes: Ensure the gradient visualization reflects physically accurate and responsive transitions when sigma is adjusted. Render using WebGL in Three.js and maintain performance efficiency by optimizing mesh and texture loading.

elements: surface plane representing zero derivative level

interactions.label: Sigma
interactions.teaches: Altering sigma changes the extent of the Gaussian, influencing how sharply the derivative peaks are distributed.

interactions.label: Highlight Derivative
interactions.teaches: Toggling highlights or de-emphasizes the derivative peaks to aid in visual understanding.

demo_steps.title: Introduction to Gaussian Derivative
demo_steps.narration: Here we have a 2D Gaussian surface with sigma=1. Notice the smooth surface and how it transitions into sharp peaks, which represent the x-derivative.

demo_steps.title: Effect of Increasing Sigma
demo_steps.narration: By increasing sigma to 3, the Gaussian surface expands, resulting in broader and less sharp derivative peaks. This demonstrates how larger sigma values emphasize broader features.

demo_steps.title: Emphasizing the Derivative
demo_steps.narration: Now toggle the derivative highlight. The peaks become more visually pronounced, helping understand areas of rapid change within the surface.

demo_steps.title: Fine Detail with Low Sigma
demo_steps.narration: Decrease sigma back to 0.5. With smaller sigma, the peaks are sharper and closer together, accentuating fine details that are blurred at higher sigma.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Tilted 3D view slightly above the horizontal plane, looking diagonally down to capture both the Gaussian surface and the sharp peaks.

inline_constraints.default_view: must match the original image silhouette, size, label scale, and crop
inline_constraints.visible_ui: at most 2 compact sliders/toggles, visible near an edge with no filled panel, and must not cover important geometry
inline_constraints.explainers: hover tooltips and click popups only
inline_constraints.framing: match the source crop and whitespace; do not force-fill the iframe if the original has margins
inline_constraints.camera: infer the source camera angle/projection/zoom so the first rendered frame is a drop-in visual replacement

interactionPlan.notes: Use color gradation effectively to highlight derivative changes; ensure transitions respond smoothly to slider adjustments. For high-performance rendering, leverage WebGL capabilities in Three.js.

Ensure that the Gaussian derivative is portrayed as a sharp peak
Remove or hide UI elements for inline representation
